papers.title: Co-precipitation mechanism of Cu-rich phase and κ-carbide precipitates in Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C-3Cu austenitic low-density steel
papers.paper_scope: Investigate co-precipitation behavior and mechanism of Cu-rich phase and κ-carbide in Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C-3Cu austenitic low-density steel, including the effect of Cu addition on κ-carbide precipitation and mutual promotion between the two phases.

processes.description: Solution treatment at 1000°C for 1 h followed by aging treatment at 550°C for 3 h
Solution treatment at 1000°C for 1 h followed by aging treatment at 550°C for 3 h

structures.overall_structure: Austenitic matrix (γ) after solution and aging treatment; presumably no precipitates (only lattice parameter measured).
structures.microstructure_counts: Austenite matrix only; no precipitate characterization reported.

structures.overall_structure: Austenitic matrix (γ) with coherent precipitates of κ-carbide and Cu-rich phase.
structures.microstructure_counts: Two precipitate phases: κ-carbide and Cu-rich phase.

phases_present.morphology: mainly irregular oval (diameter ~10 nm) and a small amount of bars (length ~30 nm)

interface_notes.stress_strain_distribution: Lattice misfit values: γ/κ: 2.3%, γ/Cu-rich: 1.0%, κ/Cu-rich: 3.4%. All below 5%, confirming coherent interfaces.

interfaces.phase_evolution: κ-carbide precipitates first from austenitic matrix. Cu atoms are rejected from κ-carbide, enriching the surrounding matrix. When Cu concentration reaches critical level, Cu clusters form and precipitate as Cu-rich phase. Cu-rich phase nucleates in austenitic matrix near κ-carbide due to lower misfit with matrix (1.0%) compared to with κ-carbide (3.4%). The presence of κ-carbide promotes Cu-rich phase precipitation. Addition of Cu increases austenite lattice parameter, promoting κ-carbide precipitation.

characterization_methods.purpose: Determine lattice parameter of austenitic matrix after solution treatment
characterization_methods.key_findings: Lattice parameter of 0Cu steel: a = 0.3687 nm

characterization_methods.purpose: Determine lattice parameter of austenitic matrix after solution treatment
characterization_methods.key_findings: Lattice parameter of 3Cu steel: a = 0.3689 nm. Cu addition increases lattice parameter.

characterization_methods.technique: High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) with EDS
characterization_methods.purpose: Examine precipitate morphology, crystal structure, and lattice spacing
characterization_methods.key_findings: Cu-rich phase: irregular oval (~10 nm) and bars (~30 nm), FCC, no obvious interface, coherent. (002) plane spacing = 0.182 nm. Almost no dislocations or stacking faults. κ-carbide: dots or bars, coherent.

characterization_methods.purpose: Determine crystal structure and orientation relationship
characterization_methods.key_findings: Cu-rich phase pattern indexed as BCC structure (Fig. 1 inset), but later analysis suggests direct precipitation as FCC. Additional diffraction spots only from Cu-rich and κ-carbide, indicating both are coherent with austenite matrix. Cube-on-cube orientation relationship: [11 0]γ//[11 0]κ//[110]Cu and (11 1)γ//(11 1)κ//(11 1)Cu.

characterization_methods.technique: Energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) mapping in TEM
characterization_methods.key_findings: κ-carbide regions are enriched in Fe, Mn, Al, and depleted in Cu. Cu-rich phase regions are enriched in Cu and depleted in Fe, Mn, Al. This indicates Cu partitioning to the matrix surrounding κ-carbide, leading to Cu-rich clustering and precipitation.

analytical_model.equation_role: Calculate lattice misfit between phases to assess coherency
analytical_model.model_note: Misfit between γ/κ: 2.3% (a0=0.3689 nm, a1=0.3775 nm); γ/Cu-rich: 1.0% (a0=0.3689 nm, a1=0.3652 nm); κ/Cu-rich: 3.4% (a0=0.3775 nm, a1=0.3652 nm). All < 5%, indicating coherent interfaces. Low misfit of Cu-rich with matrix promotes nucleation in matrix rather than on κ-carbide.

unmapped_findings: Cu addition to Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C steel increases the lattice parameter of austenitic matrix (from 0.3687 nm to 0.3689 nm), which promotes precipitation of κ-carbide.
The precipitation of κ-carbide expelled Cu atoms, locally increasing Cu concentration in surrounding matrix; upon reaching critical concentration, Cu clusters form and precipitate as Cu-rich phase.

The critical lattice parameter of austenitic matrix for κ-carbide precipitation is approximately a = 0.3670 nm; below this, κ-carbide cannot precipitate. The calculation suggests a composition boundary formula exists for intragranular κ-carbide precipitation in Fe-Mn-Al-C steels.

The paper mentions that the yield strength of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel is only 360-540 MPa (from reference), motivating Cu addition to improve strength, but no experimental mechanical testing results are reported in the current study.
Figure 1 shows SAED patterns indicating BCC structure for Cu-rich phase, but later text and HRTEM support FCC structure; this discrepancy is noted.

Figure 2 shows TEM and EDS images providing visual evidence of precipitate morphologies and elemental partitioning, but pure text mode cannot extract pixel-level details beyond captions and in-text descriptions.
